name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓمٓ (١)
```

```english
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
```

```arabic
غُلِبَتِ ٱلرُّومُ (٢)
```

```english
The Byzantines has been vanquished (2)
```

```arabic
فِىٓ أَدْنَى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ (٣)
```

```english
in a nearby land, but they, after their defeat, will be victorious (3)
```

```arabic
فِى بِضْعِ سِنِينَ ۗ لِلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْرُ مِن قَبْلُ وَمِنۢ بَعْدُ ۚ وَيَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (٤)
```

```english
in a few years. All command belongs to Allah, before this and hereafter, and on that day the faithful will rejoice (4)
```

```arabic
بِنَصْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ يَنصُرُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥)
```

```english
at Allah’s help. He helps whomever He wishes, and He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (5)
```

```arabic
وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦)
```

```english
[This is] a promise of Allah: Allah does not break His promise, but most people do not know. (6)
```

```arabic
يَعْلَمُونَ ظَـٰهِرًا مِّنَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ (٧)
```

```english
They know just an outward aspect of the life of the world, but they are oblivious of the Hereafter. (7)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِم ۗ مَّا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۗ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِلِقَآئِ رَبِّهِمْ لَكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨)
```

```english
Have they not reflected in their own souls? Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a specified term. Indeed many people disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. (8)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَانُوٓا۟ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً وَأَثَارُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَعَمَرُوهَآ أَكْثَرَ مِمَّا عَمَرُوهَا وَجَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ۖ فَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٩)
```

```english
Have they not travelled in the land and seen how was the fate of those who were before them? They were more powerful than them, and they plowed the earth and developed it more than they have developed it. Their apostles brought them manifest proofs. So it was not Allah who wronged them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves. (9)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسَـٰٓـُٔوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓأَىٰٓ أَن كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَانُوا۟ بِهَا يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was that they denied the signs of Allah and they used to deride them. (10)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (١١)
```

```english
Allah originates the creation, then He will bring it back, then you will be brought back to Him. (11)
```

```arabic
وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ يُبْلِسُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (١٢)
```

```english
And when the Hour sets in, the guilty will despair. (12)
```

```arabic
وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُم مِّن شُرَكَآئِهِمْ شُفَعَـٰٓؤُا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ بِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ (١٣)
```

```english
None of those whom they ascribed as partners [to Allah] will intercede for them, and they will disavow their partners. (13)
```

```arabic
وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَفَرَّقُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
The day the Hour sets in, they will be divided on that day [in separate groups]: (14)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَهُمْ فِى رَوْضَةٍ يُحْبَرُونَ (١٥)
```

```english
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, they shall be in a garden, rejoicing. (15)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَلِقَآئِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ مُحْضَرُونَ (١٦)
```

```english
But as for those who were faithless and denied Our signs and the encounter of the Hereafter, they will be brought to the punishment. (16)
```

```arabic
فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ حِينَ تُمْسُونَ وَحِينَ تُصْبِحُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
So glorify Allah when you enter evening and when you rise at dawn. (17)
```

```arabic
وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَعَشِيًّا وَحِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ (١٨)
```

```english
To Him belongs all praise in the heavens and the earth, at nightfall and when you enter noontime. (18)
```

```arabic
يُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ وَيُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ (١٩)
```

```english
He brings forth the living from the dead, and brings forth the dead from the living, and revives the earth after its death. Likewise, you [too] shall be raised [from the dead]. (19)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah opens with a promise from Allah, which is that the Romans will overcome the Persians in a few years after their defeat by the Persians at the time of the Surah's revelation. It then transitions from this to the mention of a greater appointment, which is the promise of a Day wherein everyone shall return to Allah, establishing the proof for the Resurrection (المعاد). Subsequently, it turns to mentioning the signs of Lordship (الربوبية) and describes His attributes, the Exalted, which are specific to Him. The Surah then concludes with a promise of victory for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and emphasizes the statement regarding it, as He says: **فَاصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ وَلا يَسْتَخِفَّنَّكَ الَّذِينَ لا يُوقِنُونَ** "So be patient! Allah’s promise is indeed true. And do not let yourself be fazed by those who have no conviction." (30:60). Shortly before that, it was said: **وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "and it was a must for Us to help the believers." (30:47).

Thus, the purpose of the Surah is the definitive promise from Him, the Exalted, to aid His religion. He preceded this with the victory of the Romans over the Persians within a few years from the time of revelation, so that the fulfillment of this promise might serve as evidence for the fulfillment of that [greater] promise. Likewise, it is used as an argument through the way of reason (طريق العقل) that He will surely fulfill His promise regarding the Day of Resurrection, about which there is no doubt.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ** "The Romans have been defeated in a nearby territory" (30:2-3). The Romans (الروم) were a race of people on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea in the West; they possessed a vast empire extending to the lands of Sham (الشامات). A fierce war broke out between them and the Persians in some regions of Sham near the Hijaz, where the Persians were victorious and the Romans were defeated. It appears that "the land" (الأرض) refers to the land of the Hijaz, and the definite article (اللام) is for specific reference (للعهد).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ** "but after their defeat they will be victorious in a few years." (30:3-4). The first plural pronoun refers to the Romans, as does the third. As for the second, it has been said that it refers to the Persians, meaning: "And the Romans, after the victory of the Persians, shall be victorious." It is also possible that "defeat" (الغلب) is a verbal noun (المصدر) used in the passive sense, with the pronoun referring to the Romans like the pronouns before and after it, so the pronouns do not differ. The meaning would then be: "And the Romans, after their being defeated, shall be victorious." A "few" (البضع) refers to a number from three to nine.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَمِنْ بَعْدُ** "All command belongs to Allah, before this and after this." (30:4). The words "before" (قبل) and "after" (بعد) are indeclinable with a *damma* (مبنيان على الضم) because there is an omitted genitive construct (مضاف إليه مقدر). The estimation is: "To Allah belongs the command from before the Romans were defeated and from after they are victorious." He commands whatever He wills, so He aids whom He wills and forsakes whom He wills.

It has also been said: The meaning is that to Allah belongs the command before their being victors—which is the time of their being defeated—and after their being defeated—which is the time of their being victors. That is, the time of their defeat and the time of their victory. However, the first meaning is more probable, if not the definitively preferred one.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَيَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِنَصْرِ اللهِ يَنْصُرُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** "And on that day the believers will rejoice in Allah’s help. He helps whomever He wishes, and He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful." (30:4-5). The adverbial phrase (الظرف) is connected to "will rejoice" (يفرح), as is His saying "He helps" (يَنْصُرُ). The meaning is: "And on the day when the Romans are victorious, the believers will rejoice in Allah's aiding of the Romans." Then He began a new sentence and said: "He helps whomever He wishes" (يَنْصُرُ مَنْ يَشاءُ) as an affirmation of His saying: "All command belongs to Allah, before this and after this."

His saying: **وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** "and He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful"—meaning, He is the Mighty (عزيز) who strengthens with His help whom He wills, and the Merciful (رحيم) who singles out for His mercy whom He wills.

Regarding the verse, there are other weak views mentioned by commentators: Among them is that His saying "And on that day" (وَيَوْمَئِذٍ) is coordinated (عطف) with His saying "before" (مِنْ قَبْلُ), and the intent is the encompassment of His sovereignty, the Exalted, over all three times: the past, the future, and the present. It is as if it were said: "To Allah belongs the command before, and after, and on that day," then a new sentence begins: "the believers will rejoice in Allah's help." The flaw in this is that it destroys the harmony (انسجام) of the verse and disconnects its end from its beginning.

Another view is that His saying "in the help" (بِنَصْرِ) is connected to His saying "the believers" (الْمُؤْمِنُونَ) rather than "will rejoice" (يَفْرَحُ), and it indicates by situational implication (الملازمة المقامية) that the victory of the Romans is by help from Allah.

The flaw in this is that its implication would be that the believers rejoice both on the day of the Persian victory and the day of the Roman victory, for in every victory there is help, and all help is from Allah. The Exalted said: **وَمَا النَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** "and help comes only from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise" (3:126). Thus, restricting the believers' joy in the help to the day of the Roman victory would be a preference without a differentiator (ترجيح بلا مرجح); so understand this.

Another view is that the "help of Allah" refers to the victory of the believers over the polytheists on the day of Badr, rather than the victory of the Romans over the Persians, even if the two victories coincided in time. It is as if it were said: "The Romans will be victorious in a few years, and on the day they are victorious, the believers will defeat the polytheists, so they will rejoice in Allah's help to them."

The flaw in this is that this meaning does not harmonize with His subsequent saying: "He helps whomever He wishes" (يَنْصُرُ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

Another view is that the "help" refers to the aiding of the believers through the truthfulness of their report regarding the Roman victory. It has also been said: The help is the dominance of some disbelievers over others, the fracturing of their unity, and the breaking of their might. These and similar views are positions not to be reckoned with.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَعْدَ اللهِ لا يُخْلِفُ اللهُ وَعْدَهُ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** "[It is] Allah’s promise: Allah does not break His promise, but most people do not know." (30:6). "Allah's promise" (وَعْدَ اللهِ) is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) whose governing verb is omitted; the estimation is: "Allah promised a promise." Breaking a promise (إخلاف الوعد) is the opposite of fulfilling it. His saying "Allah's promise" is an emphasis and affirmation of the previous promise in His saying "they will be victorious" and "the believers will rejoice," just as His saying "Allah does not break His promise" is an emphasis and affirmation of His saying "Allah's promise."

His saying: **لا يُخْلِفُ اللهُ وَعْدَهُ** "Allah does not break His promise" is like His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُخْلِفُ الْمِيعادَ** "Indeed Allah does not break His promise" (13:31). Breaking a promise, even if not inherently (بالذات) repugnant—as it might be good in cases of necessity—yet He, the Glorified, is not compelled by any necessity, so breaking a promise is never good from Him in any state.

Furthermore, breaking a promise always entails a deficiency (النقص), and deficiency is impossible for Him, the Exalted.

Moreover, He, the Exalted, has informed in His speech that He does not break His promise, and He is the most truthful of the truthful, and He is the one who says—exalted is the Speaker: **وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ** "and I speak the truth" (38:84).

His saying: **وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** "but most people do not know"—meaning they are ignorant of His affairs, the Exalted; they do not trust His promise and they measure Him against their own kind, who speak truth and lie, and who fulfill and break promises.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **يَعْلَمُونَ ظاهِراً مِنَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَهُمْ عَنِ الْآخِرَةِ هُمْ غافِلُونَ** "They know [only] an outward appearance of the life of this world, and they are oblivious of the Hereafter." (30:7). The sentence "They know" (يَعْلَمُونَ), according to what is mentioned in *al-Kashshaf*, is a substitute (بدل) for His saying "they do not know" (لا يَعْلَمُونَ). In this substitution, there is a subtle point: he substituted it and made it stand in its place to teach you that there is no difference between the absence of knowledge, which is ignorance, and the presence of knowledge that does not transcend this world. End quote.

It has also been said: The sentence is an exception (استثنائية) to explain the cause of their ignorance that Allah's promise is true, that to Allah belongs the command before and after, and that He aids the believers over the disbelievers. End quote. This is more apparent.

The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "an outward appearance" (ظاهِراً) is for belittlement (للتحقير). The outward of the life of this world is what stands in contrast to its inward (باطنها); it is that which their external senses perceive of the adornment of life, which guides them to acquire it, devote themselves to it, cling to it, and forget what lies beyond it of the life of the Hereafter and the knowledge related to it, being oblivious to that which contains their good and benefit in the true sense of the word.

It has been said: "Appearance" (الظهور) in the verse means "passing away" (الزوال), citing as evidence the verse:
*The slanderers reproached her that I love her,*
*And that is a complaint whose shame is passing (ظاهر) from you.*
The meaning would then be: "They know a passing matter that has no permanence," but this is a rare usage of the meaning.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ ما خَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** "Have they not reflected in their own souls? Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate wisdom and for a specified term..." (30:8). The intent of creating the heavens and the earth and what is between them—which is the totality of the witnessed world—"with truth" (بالحق) is that they were not created in vain (عبثاً) without a purpose beyond them, such that they exist and perish, then exist and perish, without an aim or goal. Rather, He, the Exalted, only created them for a purpose that follows from them.

Furthermore, the world with its parts is not eternal in existence without an end, such that it might be possible for every subsequent part to be the purpose for the preceding part, and every future thing to be a successor to its past. Rather, it is, with its parts, perishable and vanishing. Thus, there is an intended purpose for the creation of the world that will manifest after the annihilation of the world. This meaning is what is intended by qualifying His saying "Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them" with His saying "and for a specified term" (وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى) after qualifying it with His saying "except with truth" (إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ).

So His saying: **أَوَلَمْ يَتَفَكَّرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ** "Have they not reflected in their own souls?"—the interrogation is for expressing wonder (للتعجيب). Their being "in their own souls" is a metaphorical allusion (استعارة كنائية) to peace of mind and presence of thought. It is as if, when they are occupied with worldly affairs, striving for livelihood, and having disturbed minds, they are absent from themselves. Thus, when their minds are present, they are present and settled within themselves, and their reflection then becomes focused and not scattered, guiding them to the truth and directing them to reality.

It has been said: The intent of their reflecting "in their own souls" is that they should reflect on the creation of their own selves; that each one of them is originated (محدث), and that which is originated (المحدث) requires an Originator (محدث) who is Eternal, Living, Powerful, All-Knowing, and All-Wise. He does not create what He creates in vain, but for a desired purpose. This purpose does not return to Him Himself due to His absolute self-sufficiency (الغنى المطلق), but rather to the creation, which is the reward. This reward can only be for righteous deeds, so there must be a legislated religion (دين مشرع) that distinguishes righteous deeds from evil ones. Thus, there must be an abode in which they are tested—which is this world—and an abode in which they are rewarded—which is the Hereafter.

The flaw in this is that the sentence—I mean His saying "Have they not reflected in their own souls"—is inherently capable of being intended for this meaning, but the connection of His saying "Allah did not create the heavens..." etc., to it precludes this, as it would necessitate the breakdown of the connection due to the lack of link between the beginning of the verse and its end on this assumption.

His saying: **ما خَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** "Allah did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with truth and for a specified term" is the thought upon which they must deeply reflect within themselves. Its affirmation, based on what preceded, is that Allah, the Glorified, did not create this world, neither in its entirety nor in its parts, except as a creation accompanied by truth (بالحق)—meaning for a real purpose, not in vain without a goal—and until a specific term. Thus, nothing of it remains indefinitely; rather, it perishes and ceases. If each of its parts and the whole is a creation possessing a purpose that follows from it, and none of it is eternal in existence, then its purpose must follow from it after the cessation of its existence and its annihilation. This is the Hereafter (الآخرة) which will manifest after the passing of this world and its annihilation.

His saying: **وَإِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ لَكافِرُونَ** "Indeed many of the people are disbelievers in the encounter with their Lord." (30:8) is presented in a manner of expressing wonder, just as the verse began with an interrogative of wonder. The intent of "the encounter with Allah" (لقاء الله) is the return to Him in the Resurrection. It was expressed as "encounter" (اللقاء) to increase the wonder at their disbelief in it; for how is it possible that they begin from Him and then do not end with Him? Therefore, He emphasized it with "Indeed" (إن) to indicate that disbelief in the Resurrection is something that, in itself, should not be believed.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** "Have they not traveled through the land and seen how was the fate of those who were before them?" (30:9) to the end of the verse. When He mentioned the disbelief of many people in the Resurrection—which is a matter that renders the true religion futile—He reminded them of the state of the disbelieving nations and the evil punishment they reached, so that perhaps they might take heed and turn back from the disbelief they are upon. "Tilling" (إثارة) the earth is turning it over for cultivation, development, and the like.

His saying: **وَلكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ** "but they used to wrong themselves" (30:9)—meaning through disbelief and acts of disobedience.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **ثُمَّ كانَ عاقِبَةَ الَّذِينَ أَساؤُا السُّواى أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ وَكانُوا بِها يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ** "Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was the worst [fate], for they denied the signs of Allah and used to deride them." (30:10). This is an explanation of what the affair of those wrongdoers ended in, which is why He used "then" (ثم). "Fate" (عاقِبَةَ) is in the accusative case (بالنصب) as the predicate (خبر) of "was" (كان), and its subject (اسمه) is "the worst" (السُّواى); the predicate was placed before it to signify restriction (الحصر). "Committed misdeeds" (أَساؤُا) is detached from an object, meaning they performed evil deeds. "The worst" (السوآى) is the state that grieves its possessor, and what is intended by it is the evil punishment. "For they denied the signs of Allah" (أَنْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ) involves the omission of the "lam" of causality (لام التعليل), and the estimation is: "because of their denial of Allah's signs and their deriding them."

The meaning is: Then the evil punishment was that which the affair of those who committed evil ended in; they had no other fate because of their denial of Allah's signs and their deriding them.

It has been said: "The worst" (السُّواى) is the object (مفعول) of His saying "committed misdeeds" (أَساؤُا), and the predicate of "was" (كان) is His saying "that they denied" (أَنْ كَذَّبُوا) etc. The meaning would be that acts of disobedience led them to disbelief by denying Allah's signs and deriding them.

The flaw in this is: while it is a correct meaning in itself, the first meaning is more suitable for the context, because the context is one of taking heed and warning. What is suitable for it is the explanation of their disobedience ending in the evil punishment, not their scattered acts of disobedience ending in denial and derision, which are the greatest of them.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **اللهُ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ** "Allah originates the creation, then He will bring it back, then you will be brought back to Him." (30:11). After mentioning the proof and the denial of many people, He summarized the statement in its conclusion, which is that the beginning and the return are in His hand, the Glorified, and everyone will return to Him. What is intended by "creation" (الخلق) is the created beings (المخلوقون), which is why He returned the plural pronoun to it in "you will be brought back" (تُرْجَعُونَ).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُبْلِسُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ** "On the day when the Hour sets in, the guilty will be struck with despair." (30:12). He mentioned the state of the guilty after the setting in of the Hour, which is the hour of return to Him, the Exalted, for reckoning and recompense. "Despair" (الإبلاس) is losing hope in Allah, and in it lies all wretchedness.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ مِنْ شُرَكائِهِمْ شُفَعاءُ وَكانُوا بِشُرَكائِمْ كافِرِينَ** "None of those whom they associated [with Allah] will intercede for them, and they will disown their partners." (30:13). He means that despite their despair of mercy from the side of their own deeds, they are also despairing of their deities whom they took as partners to Allah and worshipped so that they might intercede for them with Allah, as they used to say in the world: "These are our intercessors with Allah." And they were disbelievers (كافرين)—concealers—of the worship of their partners.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَفَرَّقُونَ** "On the day when the Hour sets in, on that day they will be separated." (30:14) until His saying: **مُحْضَرُونَ** "will be presented [for punishment]" (30:16). It is said in *al-Majma'*: "The garden" (الروضة) is the orchard that is ultimate in appearance and fragrance. End quote. It is said in *al-Mufradat*: "The mark" (الحبر) is the beautiful effect—until he said—and His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **فِي رَوْضَةٍ يُحْبَرُونَ** "they will be made happy in a garden" (30:15) means they will rejoice until the mark of their bliss appears upon them. End quote.

The intent of the separation of the creation on that day is the distinction of the righteous believers from the guilty, and the entry of the latter into the Fire and the former into Paradise, as indicated by the two following verses.

The necessity of this distinction and separation in existence is what Allah, the Glorified, took as a proof for the establishment of the Resurrection where He said: **أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَرَحُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ نَجْعَلَهُمْ كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَواءً مَحْياهُمْ وَمَماتُهُمْ ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ** "Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that We shall treat them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal? Evil is the judgment that they make!" (45:21).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَسُبْحانَ اللهِ حِينَ تُمْسُونَ وَحِينَ تُصْبِحُونَ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَعَشِيًّا وَحِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ** "So celebrate Allah’s glory when you enter the evening and when you enter the morning. To Him belongs all praise in the heavens and the earth, at nightfall and when you enter the noon." (30:17-18). When He mentioned that He originates the creation then brings them back and returns them for His encounter, then separates them into two groups: the people of Paradise and bliss, and the people of the Fire and punishment—as for the people of Paradise, they are the believers who perform righteous deeds, and as for the people of the Fire, they are the disbelievers who deny Allah's signs—and He mentioned that they were in the world people of power and luxury, but they forgot the Hereafter, denied Allah's signs, and derided them until their affair ended in the evil punishment, the punishment of eradication, as a recompense for their wronging themselves; and Allah did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves.

It follows from this that in the abode of creation there is a divine governance (تدبيراً إلهياً) that is firm, righteous, and beautiful in the most beautiful way, and that for man, over the succession of times and ages, there are sins and errors ranging from evil belief regarding his Lord, taking partners for Him, and denying His encounter, to other acts of disobedience.

He concluded the speech by glorifying Him (تسبيحه) whenever a time is renewed after another, and praising Him (تحميده) for His handiwork and governance in the heavens and the earth, which is the totality of the witnessed world. Thus, He, the Glorified, is transcendent (منزه) above these false beliefs and wicked deeds, and is praised (محمود) in all that He has created and governed in the heavens and the earth.

From this, it becomes clear: First, that the glorification (التسبيح) and praise (التحميد) in the two verses are an initiation of transcendence and commendation from Him, the Exalted, not from anyone else, such that the meaning would be: "Say: Glory be to Allah" and "Say: Praise be to Allah." For His glorification and praise of Himself have been repeated in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: **سُبْحانَ رَبِّكَ رَبِّ الْعِزَّةِ** "Immune is your Lord, the Lord of Might" (37:180) and His saying: **تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى عَبْدِهِ** "Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant" (25:1).

Second, that the intent of glorification and praise is their absolute meaning (معناهما المطلق), rather than the daily obligatory prayers, as is held by most of those who say that the word "Say" is estimated. The meaning is: "Say: Glory be to Allah" and "Say: Praise be to Allah."

Third, that His saying: **وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "To Him belongs all praise in the heavens and the earth" is a parenthetical clause (معترضة) situated between the coordinated elements. His saying: **وَعَشِيًّا وَحِينَ تُظْهِرُونَ** "at nightfall and when you enter the noon" are coordinated with the position of "when you enter the evening" (حِينَ تُمْسُونَ), not with His saying "in the heavens and the earth," such that the evening and morning would be specific to glorification, and the heavens, earth, nightfall, and noon specific to praise. Rather, the times and what is in them are for glorification, and the places and what is in them are for praise.

The context indicates that what is in the heavens and the earth of creation and command belongs to Allah, necessitating by its beauty praise and commendation for Allah, the Glorified; and that for man, throughout the ages and the changing of times and moments, there is polytheism and disobedience from which His holy presence, the Exalted and Sanctified, is transcendent.

Yes, there is another consideration here in which praise and glorification overlap, which is that the times and moments, in their changing and passing, are among what is in the heavens and the earth, so by their existence they commend Allah, the Exalted. Then, everything in the heavens and the earth, by its poverty toward Him, the Exalted, its lowliness before Him, and its deficiency relative to His perfection, the Exalted, glorifies Him, as He said: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ** "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise" (18:44). However, this consideration is not what is intended in the two verses we are currently discussing.

The commentators have other scattered opinions regarding the two verses; we have pointed out the important ones among them in the views we have presented.

The change in the context in His saying: **وَعَشِيًّا** "at nightfall" is because from "nightfall" (العشي) no verb was formed on the pattern of *if'al* (إفعال), unlike "evening" (المساء), "morning" (الصباح), and "noon" (الظهيرة), where *imsā’*, *isbāh*, and *idhhār* were formed, meaning entering into the evening, morning, and noon. So it has been said.

The address in the two verses in His saying: "you enter the evening" (تُمْسُونَ), "you enter the morning" (تُصْبِحُونَ), and "you enter the noon" (تُظْهِرُونَ) is not a case of grammatical shift (التفات) at all, but rather a generalization of the address that has been directed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) since the Surah began. The meaning is:

If the matter is in this way, then Allah is transcendent when you, O assembly of humans, enter into evening, and when you enter into morning, and at nightfall, and when you enter into noon; and to Him belongs beautiful praise in the heavens and the earth.

Similar to this generalization is what appeared previously in His saying: "then you will be brought back to Him" (ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ) and subsequently in His saying: "and thus you will be brought forth" (وَكَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَيُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتَ مِنَ الْحَيِّ وَيُحْيِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها وَكَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ** "He brings forth the living from the dead and He brings forth the dead from the living, and He revives the earth after its death. Likewise you [too] shall be brought forth." (30:19). The apparent meaning of bringing forth the living from the dead and vice versa is the creation of living beings from the dead earth, then the turning of living beings into dead earth. It has also been interpreted as the creation of the believer from the disbeliever and the creation of the disbeliever from the believer, for the believer is considered living and the disbeliever dead. The Exalted said: **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً** "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light..." (6:122).

As for the revival of the earth after its death, it is the earth's recovery and its rejoicing with vegetation in the spring and summer after its dormancy in the autumn and winter. His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ** "Likewise you [too] shall be brought forth"—meaning you shall be resurrected and brought forth from your graves with a new life, like the revival of the earth after its death. The interpretation of the like of the beginning and end of this verse has preceded repeatedly.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it *hasan*), al-Nasa'i, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani in *al-Kabir*, al-Hakim (who graded it *sahih*), Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il*, and al-Diya' narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: **الم غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ** (Alif, Lam, Mim. The Romans have been defeated). He said: "They were defeated and then they were victorious."

He said: "The polytheists loved that the Persians should prevail over the Romans because they were people of idols, while the Muslims loved that the Romans should prevail over the Persians because they were people of the Book. They mentioned this to Abu Bakr, and Abu Bakr mentioned it to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him: 'Indeed, they shall be victorious.' Abu Bakr mentioned it to them, and they said: 'Set a term between us and you; if we prevail, we shall have such and such, and if you prevail, you shall have such and such.' So he set for them five years, but they did not prevail. Abu Bakr mentioned that to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: 'Why did you not set it'—I think he said—'under ten?' The Romans were victorious after that. That is His saying: **الم غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ** (Alif, Lam, Mim. The Romans have been defeated); they were defeated and then they were victorious afterward."

Allah says: **لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَمِنْ بَعْدُ ـ وَيَوْمَئِذٍ يَفْرَحُ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ بِنَصْرِ اللهِ** (All command belongs to Allah, before this and after this. And on that day the believers will rejoice in Allah’s help). Sufyan said: "I heard that they were victorious on the day of Badr."

**I say**: Regarding this meaning, there are other narrations with somewhat different contents. In some of them, the gambling (المقامرة) was between Abu Bakr and Ubayy ibn Khalaf; in some, it was between the Muslims and the polytheists, with Abu Bakr representing the Muslims and Ubayy representing the polytheists; in some, it was between the two groups; and in some, between Abu Bakr and the polytheists, as in this narration.

Furthermore, the term set was three years in some, five in some, six in some, and seven years in others.

In some, the term set initially expired in Mecca, which was seven years, so Abu Bakr extended it for them by two years by order of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and then the Romans were victorious. In others, it is contrary to this.

Then, in some, the second term expired in Mecca, and in others, it expired after the Hijrah, and the Roman victory was on the day of Badr; in others, on the day of al-Hudaybiyyah.

In some, when Abu Bakr won the bet regarding the Roman victory, he took the stakes (الخطر) from them, which was a hundred young camels, and brought them to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "It is ill-gotten (سحت); give it in charity."

What the narrations agree upon is that he gambled with them and won, and the gambling was at the suggestion of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This was justified by the claim that it was before the prohibition of gambling, as it was prohibited along with wine in Surah al-Ma'idah, which was revealed at the end of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) era.

It has been established by what we presented in the commentary on the verse of wine and gambling (الميسر) that wine was prohibited from the beginning of the Mission, and it was known from the religion that wine and adultery are prohibited.

Moreover, wine and gambling are among "sin" (الإثم) by the text of the verse in al-Baqarah: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ** "They ask you about wine and gambling. Say, ‘There is a great sin in both of them...’" (2:219). And sin is prohibited by the text of the verse in al-A'raf: **قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ وَالْبَغْيَ** "Say, ‘My Lord has only prohibited indecencies, the outward among them and the inward, and sin and undue aggression...’" (7:33). Surah al-A'raf is among the early Surahs revealed in Mecca. Thus, it is impossible that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would suggest gambling.

Even assuming the prohibition was delayed until the end of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) era, his saying (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to Abu Bakr when he brought the stakes to him—that it is ill-gotten (سحت), then his saying: "Give it in charity"—poses a problem. There is no way to rectify any of that by jurisprudential standards (الموازين الفقهية), and they have labored to justify it in ways that only increase the difficulty.

Furthermore, what is in the narration—that the Persians were idol worshippers—does not agree with the state of those people, for although they were polytheists, they did not take idols.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: **يَعْلَمُونَ ظاهِراً مِنَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا ـ وَهُمْ عَنِ الْآخِرَةِ هُمْ غافِلُونَ** (They know [only] an outward appearance of the life of this world, and they are oblivious of the Hereafter). He said: "They see the present of the world and are heedless of the Hereafter."

In *al-Khisal*, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ** (Have they not traveled through the land). He said: "Have they not looked into the Qur'an?"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَفَرَّقُونَ** (On the day when the Hour sets in, on that day they will be separated). He said: "To Paradise and the Fire."
